coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots derived from roast duck for in vitro cellular and in vivo C. elegans bio-imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Cong, Shuang
Liu, Kangjing
Qiao, Fengzhi
Song, Yukun
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: Biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via a simple and green route using duck breasts as a natural carbon source. The CDs from duck breasts were well dispersed, and their mean particle size decreased from 2.59 to 1.95 nm when the roasting temperature increased from 200 to 300 °C. Abundant functional groups such as OH, COOH, and NH2 were observed on the surface of the CDs, providing the CDs with good water solubility. These CDs emitted strong fluorescence under ultraviolet light irradiation and exhibited superior photostability. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of CDs rose from 10.53% to 38.05% when the relative nitrogen content of CDs increased from 7.18% to 12.73%. The CDs showed low toxicity to PC12 cells for prolonged exposure. Therefore, the duck CDs were successfully developed as fluorescent probes for in vitro PC12 cells and in vivo Caenorhabditis elegans imaging. These results indicated that the CDs derived from roast duck were biocompatible and can potentially be used as probes in bio-imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Roast duck
Formation
Fluorescent probes
Bio-imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo imaging of C. elegans
In vivo imaging of C. elegans

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representative TEM images of CDs formed at (a) 200 °C, (b) 250 °C, and (c) 300 °C, respectively, insets represent the corresponding high-resolution TEM images and bright-field photographs of roast duck breasts. Particle size histogram of (d) 200-CDs, (e) 250-CDs, and (f) 300-CDs. Typical AFM images of (g) 200-CDs, (h) 250-CDs, and (i) 300-CD. Corresponding height profile of CDs formed at (j) 200 °C, (k) 250 °C, and (l) 300 °C, respectively.
(a) FT-IR spectra of CDs formed at 200, 250 and 300 °C, respectively. XPS survey spectra of CDs formed at (b) 200, (c) 250 and (d) 300 °C, respectively.

High-resolution (a) C1s spectra of 200-CDs, (b) N1s of 200-CDs, (c) O1s of 200-CDs, (d) C1s spectra of 250-CDs, (e) N1s of 250-CDs, (f) O1s of 250-CDs, (g) C1s spectra of 300-CDs, (h) N1s of 300-CDs, (i) O1s of 300-CDs.
Representative 400 MHz 1H NMR spectra of CDs formed at (a) 200, (b) 250 and (c) 300 °C, respectively. The amount of CDs was 10 mg.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs formed at 200, 250, and 300 °C, respectively, (inset is partial enlargement). Three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectra of CDs formed at (b) 200, (c) 250, and (d) 300 °C, respectively. Insets in b-d show photographs of water and fluorescence CD solution under daylight and 365 nm UV light. [CDs] = 1.5 mg/mL. The maximum excitation wavelength of 200-CDs, 250-CDs, and 300-CDs were 360, 365 and 370 nm, respectively.
(a) Fluorescence decay traces and fitting curve, (b) absolute fluorescence quantum yield of CDs prepared at 200, 250 and 300 °C, respectively.
Fluorescence responses of CDs formed at 200, 250 and 300 °C with (a) time, (b) NaCl concentrations, (c) pH value, and (d) metal ions (1.0 × 10−3 mol/L).
Cell viability of PC12 cells treated with 300-CDs for 36 h. *P < 0.05 and **P < 0.01 suggest significant difference versus control group.
Fluorescence microscope images of PC12 cells incubated with 300-CDs (15 mg/mL) for 24 h. Scale bar is 25 μm.
Fluorescence microscope images of C. elegans without and with 300-CDs (15 mg/mL) treatment for 24 h. Scale bar is 100 μm.
Element contents of CDs prepared at different roasting temperatures.

head.ce:title: Biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots derived from roast duck for in vitro cellular and in vivo C. elegans bio-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Dalian Polytechnic University, Qinggongyuan1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, Liaoning, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology
National Engineering Research Center of Seafood

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Dalian Polytechnic University, Qinggongyuan1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, Liaoning, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Seafood of Ministry of Education of China, Dalian 116034, Liaoning, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Food Science and Technology, National Engineering Research Center of Seafood, Dalian Polytechnic University, Qinggongyuan1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, Liaoning, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology
National Engineering Research Center of Seafood

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared via a simple and green route using duck breasts as a natural carbon source. The CDs from duck breasts were well dispersed, and their mean particle size decreased from 2.59 to 1.95 nm when the roasting temperature increased from 200 to 300 °C. Abundant functional groups such as OH, COOH, and NH2 were observed on the surface of the CDs, providing the CDs with good water solubility. These CDs emitted strong fluorescence under ultraviolet light irradiation and exhibited superior photostability. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of CDs rose from 10.53% to 38.05% when the relative nitrogen content of CDs increased from 7.18% to 12.73%. The CDs showed low toxicity to PC12 cells for prolonged exposure. Therefore, the duck CDs were successfully developed as fluorescent probes for in vitro PC12 cells and in vivo Caenorhabditis elegans imaging. These results indicated that the CDs derived from roast duck were biocompatible and can potentially be used as probes in bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a class of carbon-based nanomaterials, have gained significant attention from various fields, such as bio-imaging [1], sensors [2], and drug delivery [3] because of their good water solubility, strong fluorescence, excellent biocompatibility, and facile method of surface modification. To obtain superior CDs, numerous synthetic methods have been explored, including the microwave-assisted method [4,5], electrospinning [6], laser synthesis [7], and hydrothermal treatment of organic compounds [8,9]. However, these synthetic routes require not only drastic reaction conditions but also the use of precursor substances that damage the environment. Owing to their low-cost and regenerative characteristics, natural resources, such as vegetables [10], milk [11], silkworm chrysalis [12], and beetroot extract [13], have been used as precursors for CD preparation via the hydrothermal method. Meanwhile, studies on facile methods of CD preparation from food consumed daily, without using any synthetic instrument, have rarely been reported.

Roast duck, one of the favorite foods in China, is rich in protein. A literature survey indicated that doping with nitrogen atoms could provide CDs with a relatively high quantum yield, which enhanced their fluorescence [14]. Accordingly, an approach to prepare nitrogen-doped CDs has been proposed. This method uses nitrogen-rich precursors, such as bovine serum albumin [15] and L-asparagine [16], unlike surface modification, which uses chemicals such as polyethyleneimine [17]. Duck meat, as a natural resource of protein, is a potential nitrogen-rich raw material for the synthesis of high quantum yield CDs. Prompted by the development of food-borne CDs from roast fish, baked lamb, and grilled hamburger [18–21], we hypothesized that duck meat would be a good precursor for the facile preparation of biocompatible CDs by using the straightforward method, including normal cooking, ethanol dissolution, water extraction and membrane dialysis.

The presence of CDs in commercial roast duck was recently confirmed in our previous research [22]. However, the formation mechanism and their potential biological applications as probes for biological imaging have not been explored. Given that precursors and reaction conditions influence the characteristics of CDs [23,24], diverse reaction temperatures may induce duck breasts to form structures of varying sizes and nitrogen-doped CDs with different quantum yields. Thus, in the current study, the effects of different roasting temperatures on the physicochemical and optical properties of CDs from roast duck breasts were investigated during the normal thermal process. The preparation of CDs from roast duck breasts was a facile method, whereas the synthesis of CDs from vegetables, milk, silkworm chrysalis, and beetroot extract, required microwave and autoclave apparatus for chemical reaction. Compared with the microwave or hydrothermal treatment of natural vegetables, the synthetic route is more time-efficient method for obtaining CDs. Meanwhile, the CDs from duck breasts were employed as fluorescent probes for in vitro and in vivo bio-imaging in PC12 cells and Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans), respectively. The results of this study are important to understand the formation of CDs derived from daily food processing and their great potential for bio-imaging.

Duck breasts were purchased from Xinchangxing Market (Dalian, Liaoning, China), and then properly thawed at 4 °C before roasting. High-glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and penicillin-streptomycin were supplied by HyClone (Logan, UT, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was provided by Biological Industries (Israel), and 0.25% trypsin by Fisher Scientific (Agawam, MA, USA), respectively. 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). The other reagents used in this study were of analytical grade.

Before roasting, duck breasts were cleaned by removing their skin. Then, the cleaned duck breasts were then cut into 3 × 3 × 1 cm pieces and roast in an oven (PO2PT, lecon, China) for 30 min (the side was turned over in 15 min) at 200, 250 and 300 °C, respectively. The roast duck breasts were naturally cooled and treated as described in a previous study with slight modifications [18,22,25]. The roast duck breasts were subjected to ultrasonic extraction with 100% alcohol (1:4, w/v) for 60 min three times. After filtering, ethanol was removed via evaporation and the residue was dissolved in distilled water. When oil was removed using chloroform, and the water phase was added with 4-fold alcohol (100%) for 24 h at 4 °C. After centrifugation (10000 rpm, 10 min), the supernatant was dialyzed using dialysis bags (molecular weight cutoff: 3500 Da) in deionized water for 72 h. The samples were then filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane filter and then freeze-dried. The obtained CDs were designed as 200-CDs, 250-CDs, and 300-CDs, and then stored at −80 °C for further use. The average yields of 200-CDs, 250-CDs, and 300-CDs were 0.02%, 0.06%, and 0.16% (calculated based on the wet weight of raw duck breasts), respectively.

The morphology of the CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEM-2100, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) (5500M, Hitachi, Japan). The particle size and height profile were analyzed using Image J and 5500Ⅱ software, respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD-6100, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) was used to analyze CD crystallinity. Elemental analysis was conducted by X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (ESCALAB 250, Thermo VG, Waltham, MA, U.S.A.), and surface functional groups were determined by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Frontier, PerkinElmer, Norwalk, CT, U.S.A.). 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy was performed on a Bruker Avance III 400 MHz NMR spectrometer (Bruker Biospin, Rheinstetten, Germany), with heavy water (D2O) as a solvent. The optical properties were characterized using a previously described method [22,26].

PC12 cells were purchased from the Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China) and cultivated in high-glucose DMEM with 10% (v/v) FBS and 1% (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C. For MTT assay, the PC12 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well. After incubation for 24 h, the cells were exposed to CDs at varying concentrations in the 0.25–4 mg/mL range for another period of 36 h. Subsequently, 20 μL MTT (5 mg/mL) was added and incubated for 4 h, and the culture medium was replaced with 150 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). After shaking for 10 min, the absorbance at 570 nm was determined using a microplate reader (Infinite 200 Multimode, Tecan, Hombrechtikon, Switzerland). Cell viability was determined using the following equation: Cell viability (%) = ODsample/ODcontral 100%, where, ODsample refers to the absorbance of the cells treated with CDs, and ODcontral refers to absorbance of the control group. For cell imaging, PC12 cells were seeded into confocal dishes at a density of 1 × 105 cells per dish. After culturing for 12 h, the cells were exposed to 1 mg/mL CDs at 37 °C for 24 h. The cells were then washed thoroughly with PBS and imaged with an inverted fluorescence microscope (Eclipse Ti, Nikon, Japan) using 361–387 nm as the excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging of C. elegans
ce:section.ce:para: The N2 (wild-type) C. elegans strains were provided by the Caenorhabditis Genetic Center (University of Minnesota, Minnesota, USA) and were maintained at 20 °C in accordance with the standard procedure [27]. L4 stage C. elegans were exposed to 15 mg/mL CDs mixed with inactivated Escherichia coli OP50 (65 °C for 30 min) in nematode growth medium (NGM) agar plates for 24 h. After treatment, C. elegans were carefully washed with M9 buffer and then anesthetized with levamisole hydrochloride (10 × 10−6 mol/L); subsequently, the C. elegans were added into a drop of M9 buffer on a slide. Fluorescence photographs were obtained using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Eclipse Ti, Nikon, Japan) at an excitation wavelength of 361–387 nm.

Physical and chemical characteristics of CDs obtained from roast duck breasts could be influenced by the roasting temperature owing to variations in the degree of polysaccharide lysis, protein denaturation, and fat oxidation in duck breasts. To confirm this assumption, the morphology and the size distribution of CDs formed at different roasting temperatures (200, 250 and 300 °C) were studied. As revealed by typical TEM images (Fig. 1 a–c), the CDs from duck breasts obtained at three different temperatures were well dispersed, with a nearly spherical shape. Clear lattice fringes with an average interplanar distance of 0.22 and 0.23 nm were observed in the 250-CDs and 300-CDs, respectively; however, no lattice fringes were observed in the 200-CDs. This finding suggested that high temperature led to intensified carbonization. The duck breasts changed in color from yellow to brown as the roasting temperature increased, also confirming the increase in carbonization (insets, Fig. 1a–c). This observation could be attributed to the Maillard reaction during roasting. On the basis of the TEM images, the average particle sizes of the CDs were calculated as 2.59 ± 0.57, 2.00 ± 0.53, and 1.95 ± 0.41 nm (Fig. 1d–f). The mean particle size of the CDs decreased and the size distribution of the CDs gradually became uniform, along with the increase in roasting temperature. The AFM results also showed that a small number of slightly larger nanoparticles (ca. 4 nm) were formed at low temperatures, whereas small particles with a uniform size were obtained at 250 and 300 °C. No apparent aggregation occurred in all freshly synthesized CDs (Fig. 1g–i). The height distributions of the CDs formed at 200, 250 and 300 °C (Fig. 1j–l) were below 3 nm, which were consistent with the TEM results. Similarly, Li et al. found the CDs produced at a low heating temperature were polymer dots without obvious lattice fringes; meanwhile, they were carbon quantum dots with a narrow size distribution and a lattice structure at higher temperatures [20]. XRD patterns (Fig. S1) showed that the CDs obtained from roast duck breasts had a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 23.20–24.22° indicating that carbon atoms were highly disordered. Moreover, the XRD peak of the CDs slightly shifted from 24.22 to 23.20° as roasting temperature increased from 200 to 300 °C, suggesting that the CDs generated at high temperatures had a greater interlayer distance (d = 0.37 nm) than that of CDs formed at low temperatures (d = 0.36 nm). This result could be attributed to the considerable greater number of oxygen-containing groups introduced to CDs at high temperatures [28].

In addition, the elemental composition and functional groups on the surface of the CDs played a crucial role in determining their properties. Therefore, FT-IR and XPS were conducted to gain further insights into the structural properties of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the peaks at 3428–3408 cm−1 correspond to the OH and NH stretching vibrations. The bands at 2944–2926 cm−1 are assigned to the CH stretching vibration, and the peaks at 1408–1402 cm−1 are ascribed to the CH bending vibration. Moreover, the characteristic peaks at 1660–1632 cm−1 attributed to the CONH2 stretching vibration are significantly enhanced with an increase in reaction temperature from 200 to 300 °C. Similar results are observed for the peak at 1598–1546 cm−1. The weak peaks at 1498–1452 cm−1 are assigned to the CN bending vibration [22,23,25,29]. These findings clearly implied that the CDs produced from roast duck breasts contained many functional groups, such as OH, CO and NH2, which were expected to significantly affect their behaviors in future applications. XPS was also used to analyze the relative element contents and exact chemical bonds of CDs. The XPS survey spectra of the CDs in Fig. 2b–d display three predominant signals of C1s at 285 eV, N1s at 400 eV, and O1s at 532 eV, respectively. Accordingly, the main element contents of the CDs prepared at different roasting temperatures are listed in Table 1 . The carbon content of the CDs decreased from 69.30% to 64.67%, and oxygen from 23.04% to 22.12%, respectively, whereas the O/C ratio slightly increased by 0.01 as the reaction temperature rose. This result indicated that the degree of oxidation of the CDs formed during roasting was affected by the temperature. That is, a higher temperature intensified oxidation. Notably, the nitrogen content of the 300-CDs increased significantly and was 1.77-fold that of the 200-CDs. This finding could be attributed to the greater amount of protein in duck breasts that was denatured and involved in the formation of nanoparticles at high roasting temperatures [11].

Optical properties play a crucial role in the application of CDs. Fig. 6 shows the influence of different roasting temperatures on the optical properties of the CDs derived from duck breasts. The UV–vis absorption spectra show a clear shoulder at 239 nm assigned to the π-π* electronic transition of aromatic CC bonds for 200-CDs and 250-CDs; however, it is weak in the 300-CDs (Fig. 5
a). Such change in absorbance was assumed to have resulted from the breakage of CC as temperature increased. The three kinds of nanoparticle solution were transparent with a light yellow color under daylight. The solutions exhibited good water solubility, as revealed by FT-IR and XPS, owing to the existence of numerous hydroxy, amino, and carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs. The 200-CDs and 250-CDs emitted strong blue fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm by a handheld laser lamp, whereas the 300-CDs solution exhibited a more yellow and distinct blue fluorescence at the same concentrations (1.5 mg/mL) (inset, Fig. 5b–d). The fluorescence spectra showed a common excitation-dependent emission behavior for all types of CDs, which was consistent with previous studies [18,20]. Notably, the maximum excitation wavelength of CDs gradually increased from 360 to 370 nm as the roasting temperature increased. This behavior could be attributed to the different functional groups on the surface of the CDs formed at different roasting temperatures, leading to diverse emitting centers [23]. These results showed that the CDs prepared from duck breasts exhibited strong fluorescence and good water solubility, which are desirable for their application in bio-imaging.

In addition, the average fluorescence lifetime and absolute fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs were measured (Fig. 6). By biexponential function fitting of fluorescence decay traces (Fig. 6a), the average fluorescence lifetime of the 200-CDs, 250-CDs and 300-CDs were determined to be 6.15, 6.66 and 7.65 ns, respectively, with a good fitted value (χ2 < 1.3). This result indicated that the excited state of fluorescence nanoparticles generated at higher temperatures were relatively more stable. Moreover, as the reaction temperature increased, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs significantly increased from 10.53% to 38.05% (Fig. 6b), which was considerable higher than the yield of CDs synthesized from other natural materials [13,33]. Doping with nitrogen has been reported to improve the quantum yield of CDs [12]. As revealed by XPS analysis, the relative content of N in the 300-CDs was considerably higher than those in the 200-CDs and 250-CDs, hence the higher quantum yield [18]. A higher quantum yield is important for fluorescent probes.

Fluorescence stabilization is an important factor for the application of CDs as probes. First, the fluorescence intensity of three CDs remained unchanged under continuous irradiation of excitation light for 30 min (Fig. 7 a). Resistance to photobleaching is a desirable property of CDs for their further applications. Second, high NaCl concentration mildly influenced the fluorescence intensity of the 250-CDs and 300-CDs; by contrast, 1 mol/L NaCl markedly decreased the fluorescence intensity of the 200-CDs from 100% to 73.16% (Fig. 7b). Third, CDs exhibited different fluorescence responses towards pH (Fig. 7c). A pH-dependent fluorescence behavior was observed, probably attributed to the different protonation and deprotonation of OH, COOH, and NH3 on the surface of the CDs in an aqueous solution. The contents of these groups in the CDs varied, which might have resulted in different fluorescence responses toward pH. Fourth, most metal ions such as Mn2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Co2+, and Ni2+ did not cause fluorescence quenching, except for Cu2+ and Fe3+, which led to photo-induced electron transfer (Fig. 7d) [8,34]. In addition, the CDs derived from roast duck breasts exhibited superior fluorescence, and particular for the 300-CDs, excellent photostability. These findings indicated that they could be employed as fluorescence probes for biological imaging.

The 300-CDs exhibited the highest fluorescence quantum yield and thus were selected as fluorescent probes in subsequent bio-imaging experiments. The cytotoxicity of the 300-CDs was assessed by MTT assay over 36 h prior to bio-imaging. Fig. 8 shows that the cell viability of PC12 cells was not affected by CD concentrations in 0–0.5 mg/mL range and only decreased by 8.81% after treatment with 1 mg/mL CDs. Thus, low-toxicity CDs obtained from duck breasts can be potentially used as probes in cell imaging.

Subsequently, the capability of CDs for cell imaging was verified in PC12 cells. Fig. 9 shows that in contrast to the control group, the tested cells exposed to 1 mg/mL of 300-CDs for 24 h emitted bright blue fluorescence, and most CDs were distributed in the cytoplasm of cells. No fluorescence signal was found in the cellular nucleus, clearly indicating that the CDs derived from duct breasts tended to accumulate in the cytoplasm. The results were consistent with most CDs generated from other sources [14]. The entry of CDs into the cells was probably due to endocytosis [35]. In addition, the morphology of the CD-treated cells was not affected by prolonged incubation. This observation strongly indicated that the CDs derived from ducks were biocompatible and caused no evident changes in the tested cells. Thus, the food-borne nanoprobes derived from our daily food could have robust potential for application in cellular imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging of C. elegans
ce:section.ce:para: The in vivo imaging capability of the CDs derived from ducks was further estimated by feeding nanoparticles mixed with OP50 to wild-type C. elegans. As shown in Fig. 10 a, C. elegans are optically transparent and unique multicellular organisms, which have primary organs, including epidermis, pharynx intestine, uterus, gonad arms, rectum and anus. Treatment of nematodes with 300-CDs at 15 mg/mL did not cause death for 24 h. Weak autofluorescence was exhibited in the intestine of worms not fed with the 300-CDs (Fig. 10b). By contrast, bright blue fluorescence was observed in the intestine of C. elegans fed with the 300-CDs. No obvious fluorescence was observed in the reproductive organs, such as the uterus and gonad arms. These observations suggested that the 300-CDs could be successfully ingested with food and mainly accumulated in the intestine. This finding gains support from other studies [13]. It further indicated that the CDs from roast duck breasts can be used as a labeling material to trace certain substances applied in multicellular organisms.

High-potential CDs were prepared successfully from roasted duck breasts by using a safe and green cooking approach, and the formation mechanism was explored. The CDs are less than 3 nm in size, and mainly possess the elements C, H, O, and N and abundant functional groups. Meanwhile, the CDs derived from duck breasts, which exhibited good water solubility, showed stable fluorescence, and a high fluorescence quantum yield reaching 38.05%. The low toxicity of CDs was observed in PC12 cells after prolonged exposure. Moreover, the CDs derived from duck breasts could be used as probes for in vitro cell and in vivo C. elegans imaging. This study proposed a facile and green strategy for preparing N-doped CDs with strong fluorescence by using normal duck meat as a precursor.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Project of China (Grant No. 2018YFD0901106, 2017YFD0400103) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872915, 31601389).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ymeth.2019.07.007.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
